source_name: Code4rena Nudge.xyz Findings and Analysis Report (2025-03)
finding_title: Unauthorized reallocation through Li.Fi executor and incomplete invalidation accounting can deplete or disrupt campaign rewards
summary: NudgeCampaign.handleReallocation trusts calls from an address with SWAP_CALLER_ROLE, but that role is granted to Li.Fi Executor, whose swapAndExecute entrypoint is permissionless. Attackers can route calls through Executor to create arbitrary or repeated reallocations. When malicious participations are invalidated, invalidateParticipations subtracts pendingRewards but does not fully restore the affected claimable/fee accounting, so repeated abuse can permanently reduce available rewards and disrupt legitimate users.
root_cause: permissionless third-party executor is treated as a trusted caller combined with incomplete reward accounting rollback during participation invalidation
broken_invariants: only legitimate user-driven swaps should create campaign participations
invalidating malicious participations must restore reward accounting consistently
available campaign rewards should not be depleted by permissionless executor-mediated calls
attack_preconditions: Li.Fi Executor or similar permissionless executor has SWAP_CALLER_ROLE
attacker can call Executor.swapAndExecute or otherwise route calldata through the authorized executor
campaign has funded reward inventory and handleReallocation accepts executor-originated calls
invalidated participations do not restore all accounting buckets used for future allocations
attacker_capability: permissionless external user with ability to invoke Li.Fi Executor; may use flash liquidity or repeated real-fund reallocations
trigger_actions: call Executor.swapAndExecute with calldata targeting NudgeCampaign.handleReallocation
trigger or wait for invalidateParticipations on malicious participations

impact.description: attackers can consume or permanently reduce available campaign rewards, interfere with legitimate reward allocation, and disrupt the campaign economic model at relatively low cost

why_not_higher: the report classifies this as Medium: impact is campaign reward depletion/disruption rather than direct theft of all protocol assets or privileged ownership takeover
why_not_lower: the exploit path is permissionless, can be repeated, and affects funded campaign rewards and legitimate user participation
common_false_positive_angles: if the authorized executor were not permissionless, the caller-validation bypass would not hold
if invalidation fully restored all reward and fee accounting buckets, the permanent reward-disruption portion would be reduced
if backend or campaign configuration restricts executor calldata destinations, arbitrary reallocation may be mitigated
mitigations: do not treat a permissionless executor address as proof of legitimate user intent
validate the actual initiator/user and swap context before accepting reallocations
make invalidation restore all relevant claimable reward and fee accounting
consider campaign-specific allowlists or signed intents for executor-mediated calls

snippets.note: report links affected call path and invalidation accounting
